title: Opening the Black Box of Elite Peer Review
subtitle: Editorial and peer review dynamics at elite general science journals

boxSteps.value: The peer review process at elite journals is often called a **black box** — papers go in, decisions come out, but what happens inside is largely hidden from view.

Thanks to an unprecedented dataset from the American Association for the Advancement of Science, we can open the box — and trace what happens to every paper submitted to *Science* between 2010 and 2019.
Inside are **68,047 papers**. Each square represents one paper. By the time they go through the review process, **only 6% are accepted to the journal**.

steps.value: **68,047 papers** were submitted to *Science* between 2010 and 2019. Each square represents a paper flowing through the review pipeline.
Every submission first enters **BORE** — a brief editorial review. About 30% (20,755) are rejected here without further review.
Papers that clear BORE may be sent to **peer review**. Only 1 in 4 BORE-approved papers (11,845) reaches this stage — the rest are rejected by editors.
Of papers reaching peer review, **4,181 are accepted** — just 6% of all submissions. The other 7,664 are rejected after review.
